nodes.name: Literary Characters
nodes.description: Famous fictional characters and the novels they appear in

items.prompt: In which novel does the lawyer Atticus Finch defend a Black man falsely accused of rape in a fictional Alabama town during the 1930s?
items.explanation:
Harper Lee's *To Kill a Mockingbird* (1960); narrated by Atticus's daughter Scout
Atticus Finch was ranked #1 on the American Film Institute's list of greatest movie heroes (Gregory Peck in the 1962 film)
Modeled partly on Lee's own father, Amasa Coleman Lee, also a lawyer in Monroeville, Alabama
Won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1961; sold over 40 million copies
The novel was meant to be Lee's only book; a second novel, *Go Set a Watchman* (2015), was written before Mockingbird but published late in Lee's life
items.cardFront: Atticus Finch (lawyer, Alabama, 1930s)

items.prompt: In which novel does the mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby throw lavish parties at his Long Island mansion while pining for a married woman named Daisy Buchanan?
items.explanation:
F. Scott Fitzgerald's *The Great Gatsby* (1925); set in the fictional West Egg and East Egg on Long Island, 1922
Gatsby's real name is James Gatz; he reinvented himself and made his fortune through bootlegging
His obsessive love for Daisy Buchanan — symbolized by the green light across the bay — drives the novel
Gatsby is shot dead in his swimming pool by the husband of a woman his car struck; Daisy does not attend the funeral
'So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past' — the famous closing line
items.cardFront: Jay Gatsby (millionaire, Long Island parties, Daisy Buchanan)

items.prompt: In which novel does the disillusioned teenager Holden Caulfield wander New York City after being expelled from prep school, railing against 'phonies'?
items.explanation:
J.D. Salinger's *The Catcher in the Rye* (1951); set over a few days in New York City in the early 1950s
Holden's central fantasy is to be 'the catcher in the rye' — standing at the edge of a cliff to catch children before they fall into adulthood
Found on the person of Mark David Chapman when he shot John Lennon in 1980; also carried by John Hinckley Jr.
Salinger became a famous recluse after the novel's success; published almost nothing after 1965
Still sells ~250,000 copies per year; frequently challenged in school libraries for language, sex, and rebellion
items.cardFront: Holden Caulfield (expelled teenager, NYC, 'phonies')

items.prompt: In which Austen novel does the clever but misguided Emma Woodhouse meddle in other people's romantic affairs in the village of Highbury?
items.explanation:
Jane Austen's *Emma* (1815); opens: 'Emma Woodhouse, handsome, clever, and rich, with a comfortable home and happy disposition'
Austen said she was creating 'a heroine whom no-one but myself will much like' — Emma is vain and often wrong
Mr. Knightley, who often corrects Emma, turns out to be the man she loves — he is 16 years older
Adapted as the 1995 film *Clueless* (Amy Heckerling), transplanted to Beverly Hills
Considered Austen's most complex novel structurally; the reader is kept in the dark alongside Emma
items.cardFront: Emma Woodhouse (matchmaker, Highbury, Austen)

items.prompt: In which Dostoevsky novel does the student Rodion Raskolnikov murder a pawnbroker to test his theory that extraordinary men are above conventional morality?
items.explanation:
Fyodor Dostoevsky's *Crime and Punishment* (1866); published serially in *The Russian Messenger*
Raskolnikov believes great men like Napoleon are justified in transgressing moral law; murdering an 'unworthy' pawnbroker will test whether he is such a man
His psychological torment after the murder occupies most of the novel; he ultimately confesses
Dostoevsky wrote it under crushing debt, sometimes dictating to a stenographer to meet serial deadlines
Sonya Marmeladova, the prostitute who urges Raskolnikov to confess, is one of literature's most sympathetic supporting characters
items.cardFront: Raskolnikov (student, Petersburg, murders pawnbroker)

items.prompt: In which Henry James novel does the independent American heiress Isabel Archer travel to Europe and fall prey to the scheming Gilbert Osmond?
items.explanation:
Henry James's *The Portrait of a Lady* (1881); considered his first major masterpiece
Isabel is from Albany, New York; she inherits a fortune and goes to Europe, believing she can shape her own destiny
Gilbert Osmond is a cold aesthete living in Florence; his manipulation of Isabel is revealed gradually to the reader
The novel ends ambiguously: Isabel chooses to return to Osmond, reasons debated by scholars for over a century
James's style — long sentences, psychological interiority, understated revelation — defines the 'International Theme' (Americans in Europe)
items.cardFront: Isabel Archer (American heiress, Europe, James)

items.prompt: In which Joyce novel does Leopold Bloom, a Jewish advertising canvasser in Dublin, wander the city on a single day — June 16, 1904?
items.explanation:
James Joyce's *Ulysses* (1922); Bloom is a parallel for Homer's Odysseus (Ulysses), wandering his Dublin 'odyssey'
Bloom is the novel's true hero: compassionate, curious, and unheroic in a way that constitutes a radical humanism
Molly Bloom's unpunctuated interior monologue — the 'Penelope' chapter — is one of the most celebrated passages in modernist fiction
Bloom's breakfast, workplace humiliations, and encounters mirror episodes from Homer's *Odyssey*
June 16 is celebrated as 'Bloomsday'; fans re-enact Bloom's journey through Dublin dressed in Edwardian costume
items.cardFront: Leopold Bloom (Dublin canvasser, June 16, 1904)

items.prompt: In which Brontë novel does the foundling Heathcliff, brought to Wuthering Heights as a child, develop an obsessive love for Catherine Earnshaw that drives the entire plot?
items.explanation:
Emily Brontë's *Wuthering Heights* (1847); published under the pseudonym 'Ellis Bell' — Emily's only novel
Heathcliff's origins (race, class, homeland) are deliberately obscured; he is described with language suggesting Romani, Irish, or mixed-race background
After Catherine marries Edgar Linton, Heathcliff returns years later as a wealthy man and systematically destroys both families
Shocked Victorian readers with its raw depiction of obsessive love; critics called it 'wild' and 'disagreeable'
Kate Bush's 1978 debut single 'Wuthering Heights' reached #1 in the UK; Bush was 18 when she recorded it
items.cardFront: Heathcliff (foundling, obsessive love, Yorkshire moors)

items.prompt: In which Alexandre Dumas novel does the wrongly imprisoned sailor Edmond Dantès escape from the Château d'If, discover buried treasure, and take revenge as the mysterious Count of Monte Cristo?
items.explanation:
Alexandre Dumas's *The Count of Monte Cristo* (1844–1846); published serially in *Le Journal des Débats*
Dantès is imprisoned on a false charge of Bonapartism; in the Château d'If he meets Abbé Faria, who teaches him and reveals the location of hidden treasure
He adopts multiple disguises and identities to execute elaborate revenge on the three men who betrayed him
One of the best-selling novels of all time; Dumas wrote it with collaborator Auguste Maquet, a common 19th-century practice
'Wait and hope' — the novel's closing message to its young protagonist Maximilien
items.cardFront: Edmond Dantès (wrongfully imprisoned, revenge, Château d'If)

items.prompt: In which Hugo novel does the ex-convict Jean Valjean, originally imprisoned for stealing bread, spend decades evading the relentless Inspector Javert while transforming himself into a benefactor?
items.explanation:
Victor Hugo's *Les Misérables* (1862); one of the great novels of French literature
Valjean is imprisoned 19 years (5 for stealing bread, 14 for escape attempts) and becomes a fugitive under the alias Monsieur Madeleine
The novel covers the political turmoil of Restoration France (1815–1832) and the Paris student uprising of June 1832
Javert, the police inspector who pursues Valjean, drowns himself when his worldview of absolute law collapses
Fantine, Éponine, and Cosette are the novel's major female characters; Fantine's story is among its most powerful
Adapted as a musical by Alain Boublil and Claude-Michel Schönberg (Paris, 1980; London, 1985), one of the longest-running musicals
items.cardFront: Jean Valjean (ex-convict, Inspector Javert, Paris)

items.prompt: In which Hawthorne novel is the Puritan woman Hester Prynne forced to wear a scarlet letter 'A' for adultery in 17th-century Massachusetts?
items.explanation:
Nathaniel Hawthorne's *The Scarlet Letter* (1850); set in Puritan Boston, 1642–1649
Hester refuses to name her lover; he is eventually revealed to be the Reverend Arthur Dimmesdale, who hides his guilt while publicly admired
Her husband, Roger Chillingworth, schemes to torment Dimmesdale psychologically rather than expose him
The 'A' begins as a mark of shame but Hester wears it with such dignity that it comes to mean 'Able' in the eyes of some colonists
Hawthorne's signature themes: Puritan guilt, hidden sin, the effects of social judgment on the individual
items.cardFront: Hester Prynne (scarlet letter, Puritan Massachusetts, adultery)

items.prompt: In which Nabokov novel does the European scholar Humbert Humbert narrate his obsessive pursuit of the twelve-year-old 'nymphet' Dolores Haze?
items.explanation:
Vladimir Nabokov's *Lolita* (1955); one of the most controversial and most discussed novels of the 20th century
Nabokov makes Humbert a brilliantly unreliable narrator; his elegant prose is designed to seduce the reader alongside Lolita
Dolores's nickname 'Lolita' has entered English as a word for a sexually precocious girl — an interpretation Nabokov rejected as a misreading of his novel
Humbert marries Dolores's mother Charlotte to gain access to the child; Charlotte is subsequently killed by a car
The novel's famous last line: 'And this is the only immortality you and I may share, my Lolita'
items.cardFront: Humbert Humbert (unreliable narrator, Dolores 'Lolita' Haze)

items.prompt: In which Heller novel does the bombardier John Yossarian desperately try to get grounded from WWII missions in Italy, only to be trapped by the rule that if you're sane enough to want out, you're sane enough to fly?
items.explanation:
Joseph Heller's *Catch-22* (1961); set on the fictional island of Pianosa off the Italian coast during WWII
The 'catch-22' paradox: a pilot can be grounded for insanity, but requesting to be grounded proves he is sane, so he must fly
The term 'catch-22' entered English as a common phrase for an inescapable paradoxical dilemma
Heller flew 60 actual combat missions as a bombardier in WWII; the bureaucratic absurdities are drawn from experience
The novel sold modestly at first but became a cult classic during the Vietnam War as anti-war satire
items.cardFront: Yossarian (WWII bombardier, Italy, trapped by paradox)

items.prompt: In which Orwell novel does Winston Smith work at the Ministry of Truth rewriting history, while secretly dreaming of rebellion against the totalitarian Party and its leader Big Brother?
items.explanation:
George Orwell's *1984* (1949); Winston works in the 'Records Department,' altering newspaper articles to match the Party's current version of history
His love affair with Julia — forbidden by the Party — is his primary act of rebellion
Room 101 (where Winston is broken) contains 'the worst thing in the world' — for him, rats
O'Brien, whom Winston trusts as a fellow rebel, turns out to be a Party enforcer; his betrayal is the novel's most devastating moment
'If you want a picture of the future, imagine a boot stamping on a human face — forever' — O'Brien's summary of the Party's goal
items.cardFront: Winston Smith (Ministry of Truth, Big Brother, Party)

items.prompt: In which Morrison novel does the formerly enslaved woman Sethe kill her infant daughter to prevent her from being recaptured and returned to slavery, and is later haunted by the girl's ghost?
items.explanation:
Toni Morrison's *Beloved* (1987); based on the true story of Margaret Garner (1856)
Sethe's act is simultaneously infanticide and an act of love — Morrison forces the reader to hold both truths
The ghost materializes as 'Beloved,' a young woman who appears at 124 Bluestone Road and consumes Sethe's life
Won the Pulitzer Prize in 1988; Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993
'This is not a story to pass on' — the paradoxical closing line, repeated three times
items.cardFront: Sethe (formerly enslaved, Ohio, haunted by Beloved)

items.prompt: In which Toole novel does the obese, pompous medievalist Ignatius J. Reilly bumble through 1960s New Orleans accompanied by his valve, his Big Chief notebooks, and his disdain for all modernity?
items.explanation:
John Kennedy Toole's *A Confederacy of Dunces* (published posthumously 1980); Toole died by suicide in 1969 at age 31
His mother Thelma Toole spent 11 years trying to get the novel published; finally succeeded with Louisiana State University Press
Won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1981 — one of only two Pulitzer winners published posthumously
Ignatius quotes Boethius's *Consolation of Philosophy* constantly; his 'pyloric valve' closes whenever he encounters anything he disapproves of
Considered the funniest American novel; set in New Orleans's French Quarter, which Toole knew intimately
items.cardFront: Ignatius J. Reilly (pompous medievalist, New Orleans, big valve)

items.prompt: In which Austen novel does the witty Elizabeth Bennet overcome her prejudice against the proud Mr. Darcy after learning she has misjudged his character?
items.explanation:
Jane Austen's *Pride and Prejudice* (1813); Elizabeth is the second of five Bennet sisters
Elizabeth initially refuses Darcy's proposal, calling him arrogant; he reforms and reveals the truth about the villain Wickham
Darcy's letter after Elizabeth's refusal is one of the pivotal scenes in all of English fiction
Anne Hathaway, Keira Knightley, Jennifer Ehle, and Greer Garson have all played Elizabeth in notable adaptations
'It is a truth universally acknowledged...' — the opening line immediately introduces the social world Elizabeth will navigate
items.cardFront: Elizabeth Bennet (witty heroine, Mr. Darcy, five sisters)

items.prompt: In which Twain novel does the runaway boy Huck Finn and the escaped slave Jim travel down the Mississippi River on a raft, evading slave catchers and con men?
items.explanation:
Mark Twain's *Adventures of Huckleberry Finn* (1884); a sequel to *The Adventures of Tom Sawyer* (1876)
Huck's moral development is the novel's center: he decides to help Jim escape even though he believes he'll go to hell for it
Ernest Hemingway called it the source of all modern American literature
The novel's use of vernacular dialect (Jim's speech in particular) made it groundbreaking and controversial
Frequently banned; the word 'nigger' appears 219 times; the novel simultaneously critiques and was shaped by the racism of its era
items.cardFront: Huck Finn (runaway boy, Jim, Mississippi River raft)

items.prompt: In which Tolstoy novel does the aristocratic Anna Karenina abandon her husband and son for the cavalry officer Count Vronsky, leading to her social ruin and eventual suicide?
items.explanation:
Leo Tolstoy's *Anna Karenina* (1878); published serially 1875–1877
Anna throws herself under a train at a Moscow railway station; the train was foreshadowed by the death of a railway worker she witnessed at the novel's beginning
The novel interweaves Anna's story with that of Levin and Kitty — a contrasting love story that ends happily
Tolstoy said his theme was that 'vengeance is mine; I will repay' — God's, not man's, justice
Nabokov called it the greatest novel ever written; it tops many critics' rankings of world literature
items.cardFront: Anna Karenina (aristocrat, Count Vronsky, railway suicide)

items.prompt: In which Kafka novella does Gregor Samsa wake up one morning transformed into a large insect and slowly become a burden to his family until he dies?
items.explanation:
Franz Kafka's *The Metamorphosis* (*Die Verwandlung*, 1915); Kafka's most read and most taught work
Gregor's first thought after his transformation is that he will be late for work — the horror is his internalization of duty even in impossible circumstances
His family initially accommodates his new form but gradually comes to resent him as he becomes unable to provide income
Gregor dies of starvation and neglect; his family feels relieved and goes on a celebratory outing
Kafka never gave the insect a specific name; Nabokov argued in his lectures it was a large beetle or cockroach
items.cardFront: Gregor Samsa (transformed into insect, burden to family, Kafka)
